speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Slate.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 3. My name is Debbi Portnoy. Today's date is May 18th, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Mr. Nathan Sobel.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I have to speed up, I realize. They made the [NON-ENGLISH] in our town. I did not hear in other places about it. Jews had to move off from non-Jewish streets. And non-Jews had to move out into their own streets. So they concentrated Jews only on Jewish streets. Subsequent to that, there was another [NON-ENGLISH] and a third [NON-ENGLISH]. They caught, the second one, 500 people. In the third one, they caught several hundred men, women, and children. And none of them came back. They were all killed. Our houses-- there also came an order we had to wear the yellow badge on the right side and the-- in the rear left, 18 centimeter in diameter. Everybody had to wear a badge, yellow badge. Our houses also had to have a half-a-meter diameter yellow badge. Every Jewish house had the yellow, big badge. I have not-- did not hear it in other towns, but we had to have it. Then came the ghetto. The ghetto came later on this year. I believe it was around Yom Kippur time. They made a ghetto. And there are two ghettos. One was for plain workers, which means they were former merchants or whatever. And the other ghetto was for artisans, people who had professions or trades, like tailors, shoemakers, mechanics, et cetera. They were in the other ghetto. Those who were in the worker's ghetto were considered to have a better chance. And they did work for the Germans. Everything was for the Germans. And they gave us-- we had certificates, which we called the golden Schein, the gold certificate. The other people had white, white certificates, white Scheins. And then they took to work whatever they needed-- on the railroad, on the-- on the roads, whatever they had to do heavy work. And we went to work in our-- in my [NON-ENGLISH]-- in my father's shop, and-- as a mechanic, and so on. They marched us every single day. And they got us. And time got worse and worse and worse. People were dying every single day. Then came October, [NON-ENGLISH], October 1, 1942 when they started killing-- the liquidation of the ghetto. All the towns around us were already eliminated. In our town, they started killing then. Before they are dead, dug four giant open pits. And they said that there was a, a, a brick factory. They're going to make bricks. But in actuality, it turned out to be the pits to, to kill the Jews. [SIGHS] Before that, we made hiding places. My father structured a special hiding place from a cellar under one room, then to a next, to a tunnel, into the backyard, under an apple tree. That's where the main, main hiding place-- and another one was a double attic in the-- over the kitchen. And half of the family was into the attic. And, and half went in the underground. And soon enough, we ran out of food, and water, and air, and everything else. And-- but somehow, we stayed for about seven, eight days. I found out later on that the first day, they killed 1,800 Jews. They took them, they got them undressed in the main synagogue, and drove them naked to the open pits, and shot them, the Germans with the Ukrainians. Second, they-- 800; the third, 600; and so on, and so on. On the eighth day, my father decided we're going to get out of the hiding place to go to the countryside. And he figured out that it was a, a, a dark night. The moon will not be out. Because the town was surrounded with thousands and thousands of militia, Ukrainian militia, which were brought down from other places. And so it was when we came out that night to-- it was the eighth or the ninth day-- to get out into a-- into a ditch, an open ditch, and run through the ditch all the way out to out of town, I lost all my equilibrium and so did the others. We couldn't even stay-- walked. We crawled on, on four. And we were like-- everything was dizzy. We couldn't-- we couldn't maintain any equilibrium. That's, that's the kind of state we were in. My grandmother couldn't go anymore. And she decided to give herself up. And the Germans must have picked her up the seventh day and killed her. My grandfather, with his daughter and two children, they ran on another night in another direction. My father's brother, and sister, and another man, they went with weapons, with knives and whatever they could, to run out of town. But they encountered a Ukrainian group. And they had a battle. And they were shot and were killed. If you-- if you fight in the-- if you yell in the desert, you haven't been hurt. You haven't fought. So they are considered as sheep. But they fought, and they died fighting. They fought against Ukrainian. They were heavily armed. My father, my mother, and the four children, we ran out the first night. And we got ourselves to the countryside. We were running from hiding place to hiding place. A lot of times, we didn't even tell the farmers. We got into the-- into the barns. We snuck in. We would steal some potatoes from the pigs. Or we would find a carrot, or potato, or whatever. We would eat. Sometimes, they would be kind. They would give us a piece of bread. Other times, they chased us away. And other times, they would even go and call the police. And we saw what they were doing, so we, we we would run and, and disappear. This was going on since October '42 through the entire winter. At one time, I remember I was frozen from cold. It was a very severe winter. And snow, snow was, like, several meters high. And I was also swollen from hunger. And we would walk at nighttime. And I would hold on with my hands on a-- on a-- on a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like, in order to help myself to, to walk. I couldn't walk. It was terrible. In the springtime, in '43, the Pole who lived in our house-- his name was Lachor-- he discovered that we were living someplace. And he came out to the countryside. And he offered to take us in to town and take care of us--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he find--

speaker: interviewee
text: --help us hide out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he find out where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Either my father told somebody to tell him-- because everybody knew my father. All the farmers, they, they loved my father. He was a very good man, a good mechanic. He would always do work and help people out. And he was a, a genius. He was known to everybody. And they knew-- everybody knew of Avrum Sojbel-- Avrum Sojbel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Lachor found out. And he came to us. He brought us a loaf of bread one day. And he offered to take our sister to him and to hide her out. So he gave-- my sister, Sarele, went with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he came back. He would come every two weeks. He would bring us a loaf of bread or something else. And then he offered us a plan, that he would bring us into town, but two by two. We would go two, two children, or my mother and father. And he would go, like, 100, 100 feet ahead of them. They would follow him. He would just sneak into town. We would walk. They believed that we were going wherever it is in the evening. And we-- and he brought us all to his place. He lives in a Jewish house. And he brought us into a barn. They had a little barn in the-- in the yard. And he brought us all into town. And we stayed there for about four months hidden in that barn. He would bring us food. And my father would do little mechanical works for him. In order they shouldn't discover us, he put in animals. He made a menagerie of animals and birds and all kinds of things that made different noises. But 20 different animals. And everybody knew that if there's noise, that oh, the animals must have made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in the barn--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barn--

speaker: interviewer
text: --together with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: --below. And we were upstairs. It was somewheres around April time, I remember he brought a-- an underground leaflet from the Warsaw Ghetto. It says, Warsaw Ghetto had an uprising. They're fighting the Germans for four weeks already. And it says, and we are trying to help them, blah, blah, blah. Anyway, at one point, he came back in Easter. He says, I spoke to my-- to my priest. And I told him that I'm hiding Jews. And I told him that, that I'm going to convert you to Christianity. And he brought some water. He says, here is holy water. And he splashed it on us. He said, till today, you were Jews of the belief of Moses. And from today, you're going to be Jews with the belief of Jesus. And now you are Christian Jews. And he brought a Bible. He says, now read it. I'm going to come to give you an examination tomorrow. You've got to know everything in, in this thing. And my father-- my father was a religious man and my mother. But we couldn't say nothing. So I took it, and I couldn't read the whole Bible. I didn't understand. I didn't know. My mind was not set for this. Came the next day to give me an examination. And he would always come late at night in the evening when people would not be around. They shouldn't see him. And, and I must have failed the examination. So he punished me. And I couldn't eat for four or five days. He would wait to see that everybody eats, they shouldn't give me any food. That was-- he was a crazy guy. And he was the leader of an underground Polish group. And, and one day--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that organization? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He called it Polish Underground. I didn't know exactly what group it was. But he would get-- be in communication with them. He had revolvers in his house. And my sister would sit in a box. On top was potatoes. And she would run inside and hide. And she would tell-- he told his little son, Jurek, that this is his cousin from the town Luck, that she's Polish and nobody's supposed to know about her. If somebody comes to the house, she has to hide. And she would cook for us and prepare-- it was not food. It was bare minimum. But-- and she was there. Then we could go down to help out with peeling potatoes or doing things, chores in the house. And my father was down in the house at one time. And then he couldn't come out because there were people coming in and out. He dealt with Ukrainians and Germans. He spoke German. He would sell vodka, and sell this, and buy this, and then this. He would do all kinds of trades with them. So he couldn't exchange with me. I was supposed to be down that day. And I couldn't go down. And my father stayed for another day. That following day, the Germans-- two German commanders came. They had about 10 Ukrainians. And they started seeking and looking. And they discovered my father and sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: --they were looking?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in July.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, they said that he's a-- he deals with the underground. And he cheated one with vodka. So he said, I'm going to show it to you. And they started, and they found-- discovered my father and sister. And they pulled them out. And I was able to see through the holes that they are taking on my father and beating him. And they say, hey, Avrum, where's your wife and your children? And they beat him up, him and my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were in the yard and all the Ukrainians around. And, and the Pole ran away. And they chased after him, and took him, and beat him up also. And I understand that they killed him the same day. And we were upstairs. And we didn't know what to do. My mother wanted to give up and my brothers. There were no more Jews left in town. There was nothing. It was-- it was almost a year after they start killing. And I said, no, we are not giving up. I don't want to face a gun. I'm not going to give up. I'm not going to make it easy. The middle of the night, we took whatever things we had. We jumped down, we ran across town. We were trying to-- and they discovered us. They started putting rockets. And they saw us running, one after another.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, partisans are running, partisans attacking the town. So they were afraid to run after us. But they, they were shooting rockets and shooting with machine guns after us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we split up. And we couldn't even find one another. I ended up with my brother. And my mother ended up with my kid brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my older brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the-- it was around August. I would say somewheres, somewheres of August, '43 already. And we went to a predestined place. I didn't even know where it was, but I was aiming, more or less, to that part of, of-- part of town, to a certain village. And we said, we're going to try and meet in this-- in this village. I forgot even the name of the village. My memory is failing me lately. And-- but we knew where to meet. And I was running around for two days and two nights, trying to find out from friendly farmers if they-- if my mother would be there. And, and finally, one told us, yeah, your mother was looking for you. She's running back and forth with your kid brother. And then we got reunited. It was around September, maybe October of '43. And some Ukrainians, some farmers would give us a piece of bread, a couple potatoes, says, go, go. Don't stay here. So we would sneak here. And it was run, run and hide and run, hide and run. At one point, I was fighting off a pig. They gave him a hot potato. So they stood on the side. When the farmer went away, I went and I grabbed the potatoes. And the pig bit me. And this is what we ate.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know who was friendly and who would be kind to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were-- before-- we were-- before the Pole picked us up, we would go to these farmers. And they would come to, to my grandfather. They would be, like, friends. They would come to town. They would stay in my grandfather's house. He would feed them. They would stay there. They would do different things, trades, sometimes sleep over even because my grandfather was from the village. So he knew these people. And then they also knew my father because he was a mechanic. And they would always come to his machine shop and mechanical shop to do different-- to have my father do work for them. And so some of them owed favors, long favors. So they, they were-- return in kind, but up to a certain point. At that point, we started running from place to place. And things got worse. The Ukrainians started fighting the Poles, and the Poles the Ukrainians. And the Ukrainians started burning Polish villages and shooting them down, the nationalistic thing. Ukrainians are very vicious nationalists, very brutal. And they would kill the entire villages of Poles and burn them down. And the Poles would go and do the same in kind to the Ukrainians. And we were caught in the middle. So we went to the fields. We were hiding in the fields. This was-- this was around September, October, November. At one point, we met up with a man which was hiding in our town. He was a refugee from Poland. And he was from a-- from a partisan group that was shot up, and they were all killed. Since he was on guard, he had a knife and a, a watch on him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he joined up with us. And he would do different works for the Ukrainians. He would make them shoes for certain, good farmers. He would make shoes for him. And they would give him a piece of bread, a couple of potatoes. This was all-- bread and potatoes was the best food ever. How did you know the dates?

speaker: interviewee
text: I made my own little calendar. I was-- I don't know how. I was able to, to look. I made a Jewish calendar and a regular calendar. The regular calendar, I knew the days. So I marked up-- I had a little, little, tiny notebook, very small, and a piece of graphite. And I held onto this piece of graphite as if it was gold. And I would mark only initials, because I couldn't spell out the entire names. It's somewheres in my boxes. We just moved not long ago. But I'll come across it one day. This is all that survived on me during the war and a belt that my father had given me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had marked up all these things. In the Jewish calendar, I was looking through the moon. I knew that it has 28 days, 29 days, that when moon is full, it's the 14th day. And I was able to count the days. I had nothing else to do. So I counted. And I made little notes who was killed when in order to remember a yahrzeit then to light a candle if and when I'll survive. I don't know. I had this mishegas in my head. And that's what I did. So I marked down those dates.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, wherever I went, I would mark down or scratch in, in fences or in houses, places of hiding. I would put down, I was here and here. And this person was killed, so-and-so. If I heard somebody was killed, I would put down the name, write it down. And, and, and I left markers all over the place wherever we went. And during the time, we were hidden in 43 or 44 places, from place to place. Then come back to him. And he would chase us out. We would run in. They would uncover us in the middle of the day. And the Germans would come. We run out in the back. And they would chase us. And we would be running someplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you left markers at all of these places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wherever I could, I left markers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of markers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Special events that occurred, whatever I heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you write it or scratch it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would write it down or scratch it in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would write special yahrzeit when they did this, and then the  Hebrew date. I knew exactly when the month was. And I would lay-- leave these markers. And it was my mother, and me, and my two brothers. Then there was Shiya, Shiya Schwarz. He was from a little town from the-- from Debenke, the other side of--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, then we started preparing. It was winter. And we knew that something happened in Russia, that the Russians are hitting the Germans hard. And they're going to start coming back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we reunited. We met some other few people which survived. But we heard that the Poles, Polish Underground-- which is called AK, Armia Krajowa, People's Army-- they're going around and killing women and children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And whoever they can find, they're just going and shooting them. Instead of fighting the Germans, those Polish underground, they are fighting-- killing off Jews and whoever survived. And they killed off several hundred people like this in our area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got together. With Shiya, we were five. Then we met another man and his wife. And he was a powerful man. He was very strong before the war. Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Iol [NON-ENGLISH]-- Iol Goldenstein. And then we met another man with his wife and two children, Chaim Laks. He was a very wealthy man from town-- him, and his wife, and two children. That was four. And two is six. And we were five. We were 11. And we made a hiding place. We would dig nighttime. In daytime, we would hide. Every nighttime, we could come, and we would-- all the villages were burned out. So that was outside of a-- of a-- of a lonely house. And we dug this hiding place for winter. And we found an old bell. We filled it up with water. And we took some potatoes. And we figured we'll live through the wintertime. And maybe springtime will be a little better. Maybe heard that they're coming back. And so we were together in this hiding place. Then one day, when the first snow came out, a man by the name of-- I forgot his name. He heard of us. And he-- and he came to us. And he said that the Poles are looking for him. They want to kill him. But because they killed already a lot of other people. There were only a few--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was Polish or Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish. Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Fischer-- his name was Fischer. And he came to tell us. But we were-- by coming to us, he made steps in the snow. And in the middle of the night, a band-- the band came to us, the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: We started-- we said, why did you do this? Why did you come? He says, I didn't know where to go and where to hide. The Poles followed him with the steps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the middle of the night, around 3 o'clock in the morning, we hear they are knocking. We had a hiding place and an open place. They're opening up and ripping the, the whole hiding place open. And then finally, he says, oh, look at those Jews, the [NON-ENGLISH]! And then he says, you better get out, or else, we're gonna throw hand grenades in! And they started ripping it to pieces. They-- we were only 11. And they were 20 or 22, with guns, and hand grenades, and machine, machine guns, and everything else-- young guys in their 20s. And they said, get out of here, you-- you Jews, you [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]! And they, they pulled us all out. The snow was a very big snow at that time. It kept snowing and snowing. By 3:00 in the morning, the snow was this high. And we didn't even have shoes, not all of us. We had szmatas and rags. And they pulled us all out from this hiding place and brought us into this little, lonely house. The 'lonely house' means there was one room. And the other side was where they kept the animal. And they had a straw roof. And they marched us all in-- into this house. And we knew that some of the Poles were holding pieces of straw, ready to light it. We knew what they were doing. They're burning-- they're burning people. And they were marching all around. And we couldn't go anyplace. There was no place to-- you couldn't run. The snow was this high. We had no-- nothing. And the moon was out. It was like total daytime, like the sun is out. And we followed one in the steps of the other. And I remember Shiya, Shiya [NON-ENGLISH] that was in the partisans. He tried to talk to them and tried to grab a gun. He knew what was going on. And he tried to grab the gun, and, and, and he couldn't. Then my mother walked behind him. And I walked behind my mother. And the others walked after me. And they drove us into this house, this little, lonely house. And they said-- and I had the watch. I was-- I was on watch at that time. And I was watching. So I had the watch. And, and then the man had the-- $100 bills. He says, here, we'll give it for Poland. Poland should be a country, blah, blah, blah, and all kinds of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the big cheese came in, the big officer. And he said-- the one that was in charge-- he said, what else have you got for us? He took off the boots of somebody else, this guy, Shiya. And he wanted the watch.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I don't know where the watch is. They realized that I was not there. But I was up here, looking down here. And I saw everybody here in this little room. And they're guarded by so many Poles with guns, and weapons, and hand grenades. Then the, the big guy, the guy that was in charge, he says, lay down on the ground! All of you, lay down! So they start screaming and yelling. Then he ran into a corner, and he grabbed a machine gun, and ddrrrr, ddrrrr, emptied out two, two, two things. And they screamed and yelled and screamed. Then there was no more screams, no more yells. And, and, and before that, they spread them out. And they said, you're going to burn. Burn them! He says, count how many there are. And they saw that one was missing. He says, [NON-ENGLISH], one is missing. He says, go seek! [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Quick, go look for him. So two guys went on top of the attic. And I was there. And I was-- saw they're coming up. And I climbed up on the wall through, through the wall. I didn't have a ladder. So I ran, and I hid myself, and I covered myself with straw and hay. And two guys came up. And they started-- one stood with the boot on top of me and looks for me. I was very small, skinny. And he's looking for me. And while, while he was looking, they start-- I saw that it started burning. They started yelling, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]! Quick, quick, it's burning!

speaker: interviewer
text: So they jumped down and jumped out of the house. And the house was all on fire. And I'm sitting there, and the dead people are here. And the house is burning. And I watch the, the straw burn.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everything is straw and hay. And the fire's coming to me. And I'm sitting, and I don't know what, what, what to do. I was sitting there till it started burning on me. Then I jumped down quickly. And I broke a window, and I jumped out. At that point, they heard a window breaking. So they ran back. They were chasing me. They tried to catch me. I ran into the fire, into the smoke. And I, I don't know whether the saw me or not, but I covered my face with a coat. And I was just staying there for another five minutes till I heard that they left with their wagons. There were wagons there. And they-- and they, they took off. That's when I ran out to another hiding place. And I stood there. And I thought-- I woke up in the morning. I thought maybe it was a nightmare. Then I realized that my mother's not here, nobody's here. I am the only one. I realize that, that this is the truth. I walked out, and I still saw the house burned. Then I went back to our hiding place, and I started looking for a knife, or something to, to, to just go wander or give myself up. It was on my birthday, in January, 1944. The Russians were already close to, to-- not far. You could hear the, the, the cannons coming back. And, and, and I didn't know whether anybody's still alive. I had no communication with anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went back to this hiding place. And I hear a, a voice in Yiddish. And I saw blood. And I go in. And I hear a voice, oy, [YIDDISH]. This is-- my kid brother's name is Bentzion. Says, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]? Where do you come from? Then he says-- then I ask him where he came from.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then finally, I realize that he is shot. This guy, Iol Gottenstein-- we called him Iol [NON-ENGLISH]. And he sustained eight bullets in his body. Everything-- both hands were broken. His chest was filled with bullets. The only thing-- the head and the feet did not have any bullets. And I realize that he is all bloodied up. And he told me what happened. He tried to wake my mother up. She was dead, he said. On to the others-- they were all lying there. And he threw-- with his head, he broke the window and threw himself out of the house. And he climbed back to this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took him back to this other hiding place. And there was just room for, for the two of us-- small, tiny place. And I still stayed with him for six full days. I put rags on him, with pieces of bread, with potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have any medication. And I didn't sleep all this time. And I would just give him a rope in his mouth, and lift him up a little bit, and let him down. Lift him and let him down. He would sing and pray. And he would cry and scream. He would pray the whole davening from Yom Kippur davening. He would count to a million and count back, just to kill time. He was suffering. He was struggling. And, and he started-- the body started smelling. The wounds were smelling. It was the sixth or fifth day.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of a sudden, he's talking to, like, my mother. And he said, so what do you say, you'll remain alive? Oh, oh, I see. And it was like he was talking to my mother. And he says, oh, your mother was here. She says you'll be the only lucky child to remain alive. And I said, what are you talking about? Then he says, take me out of here. Put it outside, so it should be easier for you to, to bury me, because your mother said I have another day to live. In the morning, he's talking, like, to my father. And he said-- he said, so you-- [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. What do you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, your father was here. And he-- [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. We're making a wedding. You are not here. You are the only one who's missing. And he keeps talking, talking. And I couldn't hear this-- this-- I was afraid, and, and all that. Finally, he said, look-- he lived through the whole night. It was the longest night of my life. In the morning, he says, I want a little fresh water before I die. Your father said I only have-- I have only a few hours to live. And I want a little bit of fresh water.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told him, Iol, it's Sunday morning. Please, if the-- I go out, they'll, they'll come, and they'll kill us. He said, I don't care, I want a little fresh water before I die. And he told me who was killed here and who was killed there, what is-- he was hiding things over there. He told me the whole history all in-- on, on, on a shoestring in, in two minutes. So I said, OK, if he's screaming and all this, I better go and get some water. So I run to this place where they killed everybody what was burned down. There was the well. And there was ice around it. And I pulled out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was in the-- I did not look at the bones before. It was the first time in-- it was the sixth day. I pulled out the water. And I'm looking at the water. I'm looking at the-- at the bodies. And I hear the noise. All of a sudden, I see two bad guys are standing about 10, 15 feet away, with Poles-- four, five, six people here and here with guns. And they start screaming, halt, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], stop, stop! And they start chasing me. And I ran, bent down. And I ran in a zigzag fashion. And they're shooting. And I sustained a bullet to my foot. And they chased me. And I ran into this hiding place. And I said, Iol, here's the water, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. Here, you don't need it anymore. Don't tell them where I am. And I ran into this other little hiding place next to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue this story on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]
